steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all possible pairs of elements and check if their sum equals the target. This approach is naive and inefficient for large inputs.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Sort the input array first, then use two pointers to find the pair that sums up to the target value efficiently.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each pair of elements for data type compatibility. Brute force by iterating through pairs.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Track the most common data type encountered during a single pass through the array.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: The brute force approach directly applies if/else statements to handle all possible conditions, no optimization needed.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Optimal solution involves using if/else statements effectively to handle different cases with minimal checks.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Evaluate all cases sequentially until a match is found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a dictionary to map keys directly to values for constant-time lookup.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Arrays are collections of elements stored contiguously in memory. Strings are arrays of characters with a null terminator.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the array with two nested loops to compare each element with every other element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a single loop to find the maximum value by keeping track of the highest element encountered so far.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and compare it with every other element to find duplicates.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Sort the array first, then iterate through it once to check for consecutive duplicates.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each function call to determine if it passes by value or reference.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Core concept: Understand that in most languages, functions pass parameters either by value (for primitive types) or by reference (for objects). This insight helps in analyzing function behavior and optimizing code accordingly.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and compare it with every other element to find the maximum or minimum.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Traverse the array once, keeping track of the current maximum or minimum value encountered.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each digit of the number and count them one by one.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Count digits by repeatedly dividing the number by 10 until it becomes zero. Each division reduces the problem size by a factor of 10.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert number to string, reverse the string, convert back to number.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Extract digits using modulo and division, construct reversed number iteratively.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert the number to a string and compare it with its reverse.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Reverse only half of the number and compare it with the first half.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all numbers up to the smaller of the two given numbers to find their common divisors and compute the greatest one.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use Euclid's algorithm, which repeatedly replaces the larger number by its remainder when divided by the smaller number until one of the numbers becomes zero. The other number at that point is the GCD.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all numbers up to d^d where d is the number of digits. For each number, calculate the sum of its digits raised to the power of d and check if it equals the number.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Determine the number of digits in the number, then repeatedly extract and process each digit without needing to calculate powers for all numbers up to a large limit.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate from 1 to n and check if each number divides n without a remainder.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Iterate from 1 to √n, checking divisors on both sides of the square root for efficiency.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check every number from 2 to n-1 if it divides n. If any do, n is not prime.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Only check divisors up to the square root of n. If any divisor is found in this range, n is not prime.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Recursively calls itself N times, doubling the call stack with each recursion.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Utilizes a loop internally to avoid multiple recursive calls, maintaining constant space complexity.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Recursively call a function to print numbers from 1 to N. Each recursive call adds a layer to the stack.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use an auxiliary variable to keep track of the current number, incrementing it in each recursive call until it reaches N.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate from N to 1 using a loop. This approach uses an iterative method with linear time complexity and space complexity due to the stack used for function calls.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use recursion to print N to 1. The recursive approach directly mirrors the problem statement, with a constant space complexity due to tail recursion optimization or iterative implementation if necessary.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iteratively calculate sum using a loop. Each recursive call adds to the stack.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use the formula for the sum of first N natural numbers: (N * (N + 1)) / 2.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Calculate factorial iteratively or recursively without memoization.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use recursion with memoization to avoid redundant calculations.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Reverse the array using an auxiliary stack to hold elements temporarily.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Swap elements from both ends towards the center until they meet in the middle.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Reverse the string and compare with the original. Convert to list for reversal.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers, one from start and one from end, move towards center comparing characters.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Recursively calculates Fibonacci numbers without memoization.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Uses dynamic programming to store previously computed values for efficiency.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Simply compare each element with every other element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hash table to store and quickly check for the existence of elements.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and count its occurrences by comparing with every other element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hash map to store the frequency of each element as you iterate through the array, resulting in linear time complexity.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and count its frequency using another loop. Track the highest and lowest frequencies.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hash map to store the frequency of each element in one pass. Iterate through the hash map to find the highest and lowest frequencies.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and find the smallest element in the remaining unsorted part, then swap it with the current element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Same as brute force but optimized by reducing unnecessary swaps. Track the index of the minimum value and perform a single swap at the end of each pass.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Compare adjacent elements and swap them if they are in the wrong order. Repeat this process n times.

steps.label: optimal
steps.tc: O(n) (best case when array is already sorted)
steps.desc: After each pass, the largest unsorted element moves to its correct position. Stop if no swaps are needed in a pass.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and compare it with its preceding elements to find the correct position, then insert it.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Same as brute force but optimized by breaking early if no more swaps are needed.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Compare each pair of adjacent items and swap them if they are in the wrong order. Repeat this process for each item, moving unsorted elements towards the end.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Optimize by adding a flag to detect if any swaps occurred during a pass. If no swaps, the array is already sorted and can terminate early.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Insert each element into its correct position by comparing it with all previously sorted elements. Repeat until the entire list is sorted.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Optimization: For each element, only compare and swap if needed, reducing unnecessary swaps and comparisons.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the array and keep track of the maximum element found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Initialize the maximum element with the first element, then iterate through the array comparing each element with the current maximum and updating it if necessary.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and compare it with all other elements to find if it's larger or smaller.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Maintain two variables, one for the largest and one for the second largest. Update these variables as you iterate through the array.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Compare each element with all subsequent elements to check if they are sorted.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Iterate through the array and compare each element with its next one; if any pair is out of order, return false. Otherwise, return true.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the array and shift each element to its left by one position manually.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a temporary variable to store the first element, then shift all other elements to the left, and finally place the stored element at the end of the array.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Rotate the array by one position d times.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Reverse the entire array, then reverse first d elements and last n-d elements separately.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element of the array until the target is found or the end is reached.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Check each element in the array sequentially until the target is found.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Merge the two arrays into a new array and sort it.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers to traverse both arrays, comparing elements and appending the smaller one to the result. Move the pointer of the array with the smaller element.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible subarrays and check if their sum equals K. Keep track of the longest valid subarray.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a sliding window approach to maintain a window that sums up to K. Adjust the window's start and end based on the sum value.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the array and swap any misplaced positive or negative number with the next one. Repeat until no more swaps are needed.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Rearrange the array in-place using a pointer to track misplaced elements and another to iterate through the array, ensuring positive and negative numbers alternate by swapping when necessary.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element if it is greater than all elements after it. This involves nested loops.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Traverse the array from right to left, keeping track of the maximum element seen so far. Compare current element with this max and update if necessary.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all possible subarrays and check if their sum equals K.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hashmap to store the cumulative sum up to each index, allowing constant-time lookups for required sums.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible quadruplets and check if their sum equals the target.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Sort the array, then use two nested loops to fix two elements and use a two-pointer technique to find the other two elements that make up the sum.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all possible subarrays and calculate their sums to find the largest subarray with a sum of zero.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hash map to store the cumulative sum up to each index. If the same cumulative sum is encountered again, it means the subarray between these indices sums to zero.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible subarrays and count those whose XOR equals K.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a hash map to store the prefix XOR and its frequency. Iterate through the array, calculate the current XOR, and check if there exists a previous prefix XOR such that their difference equals K.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Merge by copying both arrays into a new array and then sorting it.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Reverse merge from the end of the arrays, effectively shifting elements in place to avoid extra space.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element sequentially to find the lower bound.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Utilize binary search to efficiently locate the smallest index where the element is greater than or equal to a target value.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element until finding the target or reaching the end.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Utilize binary search to efficiently find the upper bound by narrowing down the search range based on comparisons.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the array to find the floor and ceil values. Compare each element with the target.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search to efficiently locate the floor and ceil values in a sorted array by comparing middle elements with the target.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element sequentially to find the first and last occurrence.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search to efficiently narrow down the range, reducing the search space by half in each step.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Count occurrences by iterating through the array and incrementing a counter when the target value is found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search to find the leftmost and rightmost indices of the target in the sorted array, then calculate the number of occurrences by subtracting these indices.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element in the array to see if it matches the target. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search with modifications to handle duplicates, where you ignore one half of the array when both ends are equal to the middle element.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element with its previous one to find a break where the next is smaller than the current.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search: Compare middle element with last. If mid < last, rotation point is in left half; otherwise, in right half.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iteratively incrementally approach the root by checking each number. Continue until the nth power is close to the target.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search between 0 and the target number. Adjust the mid-point based on whether its nth power is greater or less than the target, narrowing down to the root.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each day from 1 to d to see if we can make M bouquets. For each day, count how many bouquets can be made.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search between the minimum and maximum possible days to make M bouquets. For each mid-day, count if we can make M bouquets.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each number from 1 to n if it divides all numbers up to m. The first such number is the answer.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search on the range [1, m] to find the smallest divisor of n. Check if a mid-point divides n; adjust the search range accordingly.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each number starting from 1 and count missing positives until the k-th one is found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search through the sorted array to find the range where the k-th missing positive number lies. Adjust the search based on the count of missing numbers in that range.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Try placing each cow at every possible position and check if it's possible to place all cows with the given minimum distance.

steps.label: optimal
steps.tc: O(n log n + n log m)
steps.desc: Sort the positions, then use binary search on the possible distances between cows. Check feasibility for each mid-point in binary search.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all possible allocations of books to students and find the minimum number of pages among those that satisfy the condition.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search on the number of pages a student can read. Determine if it's possible to allocate books in such a way that no student has more than 'mid' pages, where 'mid' is the middle value between the maximum pages and the sum of all pages.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Divide the array into all possible pairs and calculate their sums. Compare each sum to find the pair with the largest sum.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search for the maximum possible sum by guessing a potential sum and checking if it can be achieved by splitting the array into non-empty subarrays. Adjust the search range based on whether the guessed sum is achievable.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Divide the wall into n segments and try all combinations to find the minimum height.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Binary search for the minimum height. Set lower bound as the maximum wall height, upper as the sum of all wall heights divided by n segments.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all possible placements of gas stations and calculate the maximum distance to any station for each placement.

steps.label: optimal
steps.tc: O(n log n + n log m)
steps.desc: Sort gas stations, then use binary search on the possible distances between stations to find the minimum max distance.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Merge both arrays into a single sorted array and return the k-th element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search to find the k-th smallest element by partitioning both arrays optimally.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each row and count the number of 1s. Track the row with the maximum count.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Start from the top-right corner and move left or down based on the value, efficiently finding the row with the most 1s.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each element and compare it with its neighbors to find a peak. If an element is greater than all of its neighbors, it's a peak.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Treat the 2D matrix as a 1D array and perform binary search. For each middle element, check its neighbors to decide which half of the matrix to explore next.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Sort all elements of the matrix in a single array and find the median.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use binary search to find the median by counting elements less than or equal to a mid value within each row.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each character and check if it's part of an outermost parenthesis by counting nested parentheses. Remove if outermost.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Track the depth of nested parentheses using a counter. Ignore characters when counter is at 0 or 1, otherwise add to result.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Split the string into words using a simple loop, then reverse each word individually. Concatenate them back with spaces.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Reverse the entire string first, then reverse each word to get the correct order of words and characters within each word.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert each character to an integer and check if it's odd. If so, compare it with the current largest odd number found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Scan the string from right to left. Stop at the first odd digit and return it as the largest odd number in the string.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all characters of each string until a mismatch is found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Compare the shortest string with others to find the common prefix efficiently.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each character of both strings and compare their occurrences.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two hash maps to map characters from one string to another and vice versa, ensuring one-to-one correspondence.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Rotate each string of length n by all possible rotations (n) and check if any rotation matches the other string.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Concatenate the first string with itself, then check if the second string is a substring of this concatenated string.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Count character frequencies and sort characters by frequency. Brute force due to sorting.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a max-heap (priority queue) to place characters based on frequency, then build the result string from heap elements.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each substring to determine if it is valid and keep track of the maximum nesting depth.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Iterate through the string, maintaining a counter for the current depth. Increment when encountering an opening parenthesis '(' and decrement when encountering a closing parenthesis ')'. Track the maximum depth encountered.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert each Roman numeral to its integer equivalent and sum them up. Iterate through the string to find matching substrings like 'IV' or 'IX'.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Iterate through the Roman numeral string from left to right, comparing each character with the next one. If the current value is less than the next, subtract it; otherwise, add it.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert integer to string and then use a lookup table for each digit.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a fixed array of Roman numeral symbols and their values, iterating through the integer from largest to smallest value.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each character of the string, converting it to an integer while handling signs and overflow.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Optimize by using a loop to process digits one by one, checking for signs, and updating the result carefully to handle overflow conditions.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all substrings and count those with exactly k distinct characters.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a sliding window to track the number of distinct characters in each substring, expanding and contracting as needed.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Calculate beauty for each substring by checking all possible substrings and computing their beauty.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Compute the beauty of each substring in linear time using a sliding window approach to efficiently calculate the number of unique characters.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each bracket and try to balance it with every other bracket. This leads to a quadratic time complexity.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a counter to keep track of open brackets. For every close bracket, if there's an unmatched open bracket, increment the counter; otherwise, decrement it. The final count gives twice the minimum number of reversals needed.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each digit and compare it with the next to count consecutive occurrences. Store counts in a new string.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers to traverse the string, counting consecutive identical characters and appending the count and character to the result string.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Naive approach that slides a window of size m over the string and compares each substring with the pattern. It checks for equality character by character.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Rabin-Karp algorithm uses hashing to efficiently find if a pattern exists in a string. It computes hash values of the current window and the pattern, then checks for equality. If hashes match, it performs a character-by-character comparison.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Compute the Z-function for each position by comparing characters with a sliding window.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Efficiently compute the Z-function using dynamic programming and two pointers to track the current segment of the string that is known to be matched.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible substrings and check if they are palindromes. For each substring, try to insert characters at the beginning or end to make it a palindrome.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use dynamic programming to build a table that stores whether a substring is a palindrome. Iterate over all substrings and fill the table accordingly, ensuring minimal insertions by comparing characters at both ends.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible suffixes of the string and check if they form a palindrome when prepended to the original string.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) algorithm to find the longest palindromic prefix, then append the reverse of the remaining suffix to make the entire string a palindrome.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all prefixes of the string against all suffixes to find the longest prefix that is also a suffix.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use KMP algorithm's preprocessing step (partial match table) to efficiently find the longest proper prefix which is also a suffix in linear time.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all possible substrings and check if each is a palindrome.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Expand around center for both odd and even length palindromes.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to count nodes and find middle element.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers, fast and slow. Fast moves twice as fast as slow; when fast reaches end, slow is at middle.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate to find the position before the insertion point and insert. Traverse the list until the correct position is found.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Directly insert the new node after a given node, requiring only a few pointer manipulations.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to find the node before the target and then update pointers.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Copy the value of the next node to the current node and bypass the next node.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the linked list once, counting nodes until reaching the end.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers moving at different speeds (e.g., one twice as fast). When the faster pointer reaches the end, the slower pointer will be at the middle, which helps in calculating length.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the linked list from head to tail until the target element is found or the end of the list is reached.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Similar to brute force, but use a pointer to traverse the list, which may be slightly optimized for some hardware architectures.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the list, for each node, traverse again to find its predecessor and successor, then swap them.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Traverse the list once, for each node, directly swap pointers of the current node with its predecessor and successor.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to find the position where the new node should be inserted, then insert it. Traverse time is linear.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Update the pointers of the existing nodes adjacent to the insertion point without traversing the list.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to find the node and then update the pointers of its previous and next nodes. This approach requires a single pass through the list.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Update the pointers of the given node's previous and next nodes to bypass the current node, effectively deleting it from the list without needing traversal.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through the list and swap each node's pointers to reverse it.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Traverse the list, reversing each node's pointers in-place without using extra space.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each node against every other node to find if they are the same. This involves nested loops.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm. Move one pointer (tortoise) by one step and another pointer (hare) by two steps until they meet. Then, move one pointer to the start and keep both moving until they meet again, which gives the loop length.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to find its length and then compare elements from the start and end moving towards the center.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a slow and fast pointer to find the middle, reverse the second half, and compare it with the first half. Restore the list if needed.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each node and move it to the end of the list if it's even.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Maintain two pointers, one for odd nodes and one for even nodes. Traverse the list, appending nodes to their respective lists, and finally connect them.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the linked list to find the middle node and then delete it.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two pointers, one moving twice as fast as the other. When the faster pointer reaches the end, the slower pointer will be at the middle node, which can then be deleted.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each node and for each, compare it with every other node to find its correct position. Swap nodes until the list is sorted.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Divide the list into halves recursively until each sublist has one or zero elements. Merge these sublists back together in a way that sorts them, resulting in a fully sorted list.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Sort the linked list using a comparison-based sorting algorithm like merge sort or quicksort.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a three-pointer technique to partition the list into nodes with 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass through the list.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the linked list to find the last node that is not a 9 and increment it. If all nodes are 9s, add an additional node with value 1 at the beginning.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Traverse the linked list from the end to the beginning, incrementing each node until a non-9 node is found. If all nodes are 9s, continue traversing and set each node to 0 until reaching the first non-9 node or the head, then increment that node by 1.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse the list to find the nth node from the end, then detach and attach it to the beginning.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Calculate the length of the list, determine the new head position, adjust pointers accordingly to rotate the list in one pass.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Traverse through each node and for each node with a child, traverse to the end of its child list, then link it back to the original list. Repeat this process.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use an iterative approach with three pointers: current, tail, and next. Traverse the list and for each node with a child, link the current node's next to the child, then find the end of the child list and connect it back to the original list. Move the current pointer to the next node.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iteratively convert each character to its integer value and accumulate the result.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Recursively process each character, updating the result by multiplying the accumulated value by 10 and adding the new digit's integer value.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check all numbers from 1 to n. For each number, check if it is a good number by verifying its prime factors.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use Sieve of Eratosthenes to find all prime numbers up to n. Then, use these primes to determine which numbers are good by checking their prime factors.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Reverse the stack, then insert each element into a new sorted stack using a helper function.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Recursively sort the stack by removing its top element and sorting the rest. Then, insert the top element back into the sorted stack in the correct position.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Reversing a stack using recursion can be done by popping the top element and recursively reversing the remaining stack. Then, insert the popped element at the bottom of the reversed stack.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Optimize the reversal process by using an auxiliary function to insert an element at the bottom of the stack. This reduces the time complexity compared to the brute-force method.

steps.label: brute

steps.label: brute

steps.label: brute

steps.label: brute

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all subsets using recursion and store unique ones.

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all combinations of numbers from 1 to 9 and check if their sum equals k. Use recursion to explore all possibilities.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use backtracking to explore combinations of numbers from 1 to 9. Recursively build combinations and backtrack when necessary to find valid ones that sum to k.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all combinations by recursively iterating through each digit's possible letters and concatenating them. The time complexity is exponential due to the branching factor of 4 (except for '7' and '9').

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a backtracking approach to explore all possible combinations. This method generates each combination in a single path of recursion, optimizing space complexity by avoiding the need for additional data structures like arrays or strings.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Try every cell as a starting point and explore all four directions for the word, using recursion.

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Try every possible way to break the word by checking each prefix against a set of words. Recursively explore all combinations.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use dynamic programming to store results of subproblems and avoid redundant computations. Iterate through the word, checking for valid breaks and building up solutions.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Try all possible color combinations for each vertex. Exponential in time due to high branching factor.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use backtracking, pruning invalid colors and only exploring valid ones. Efficiently handles constraints through recursion.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Try all possible operators (+, -, *, none) between numbers. This generates all combinations and evaluates them.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use dynamic programming to store intermediate results of adding or subtracting the last number. Recursively explore all possible expressions until reaching the end.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each bit from right to left until an unset bit is found and set it. Traverse the entire bit length of the number.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a bitwise OR operation with its two's complement to flip the rightmost unset bit. This approach modifies only the necessary bit without iterating through all bits.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Generate all subsets by iterating through each element and deciding whether to include it or not. Each subset can be represented as a binary number.

steps.label: brute

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each number from 1 to n if it divides the given number.

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each number from 2 to n if it divides the given number. If it does, divide and continue checking.

steps.label: optimal

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Use two queues to simulate stack operations. Enqueue elements into the first queue and transfer all but one element to the second queue, then swap them. This approach is inefficient for multiple push and pop operations.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use one queue. For push, enqueue the element to the queue. For pop, dequeue all elements except the last one, then dequeue and return the last element. For peek, perform the pop operation but re-enqueue the popped element before returning it.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Using two stacks. Enqueue by pushing to stack1. Dequeue by popping all elements from stack1 to stack2, then pop the top of stack2.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Maintain two stacks. Push to stack1 for enqueue. For dequeue, if stack2 is empty, transfer all from stack1; then pop from stack2.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Use a linked list to store elements. Each element points to the next in line, making operations like push and pop efficient.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Maintain a pointer to the top of the stack. Perform all operations (push, pop, peek) by adjusting this pointer and updating linked list nodes.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Use an array to store elements. Enqueue operation appends at the end and has O(1) time complexity. Dequeue operation removes from the front, which can require shifting all other elements forward, resulting in O(n) time complexity.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a linked list where each node has a reference to the next node. Enqueue operation inserts at the tail, and dequeue operation removes from the head, both with O(1) time complexity.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Converts an infix expression to postfix using a stack. The brute force approach involves scanning the expression and deciding whether to push operators onto the stack or directly output them based on precedence and associativity rules.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: The optimal approach uses a stack to store operators during conversion. It processes each character of the infix expression, pushing operands directly into the output and applying operator precedence rules using the stack.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert prefix to infix by iterating from left to right and using a stack. For each character, if it's an operator, pop two elements from the stack, create a sub-expression, and push it back onto the stack.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep track of operands. When encountering an operator, pop the required number of operands from the stack, create a sub-expression with the operator and operands, and push the result back onto the stack.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert prefix to infix first using a stack, then convert infix to postfix. Infix conversion is O(n^2) due to nested loops.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two stacks: one for operators and one for operands. Traverse the prefix string from right to left, pushing numbers onto the operand stack and applying operators to the top elements of both stacks when an operator is encountered.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert postfix to infix first, then convert infix to prefix. Infix conversion takes O(n^2) due to nested loops, while prefix conversion is linear.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep operators and operands, pushing them onto the stack as you iterate through postfix. When encountering an operator, pop the required number of operands from the stack, apply the operator, and push the result back onto the stack. The final result will be the only element left in the stack.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert postfix to infix using a stack. For each token in postfix, push onto the stack if it's an operand. If it's an operator, pop two operands from the stack, create an expression with the operator and operands, and push the new expression back onto the stack.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a single stack to build the infix expression. For each token in postfix, if it's an operand, push it onto the stack. If it's an operator, pop two operands, create an infix expression with parentheses around them and the operator, and push this new expression back onto the stack.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Convert infix to postfix first using a stack. Then convert the postfix expression to prefix by reversing it and applying the same rules used for postfix to prefix conversion.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use two stacks: one for operators and one for operands. Iterate through the expression from right to left, pushing operands directly to the operand stack and applying rules based on operator precedence and associativity.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Check each element and find the next greater element by scanning the entire array from that point.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep track of elements for which we haven't found the next greater element yet. Iterate through the array twice.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Compare each element with all subsequent elements to find the next smaller element. This results in a nested loop, leading to quadratic time complexity.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep track of elements for which we haven't found the next smaller element yet. For each new element, pop from the stack until the stack is empty or the top of the stack is greater than the current element.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: For each element, count how many elements to its right are greater than it by iterating through all elements to its right.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep track of elements and their counts of NGEs. Iterate from the end, pushing elements onto the stack if they are less than or equal to the current element; otherwise, pop elements and count until finding a greater one, then push the current element with its count.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all subarrays and find the minimum of each. Sum these minimums.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a stack to keep track of indices in increasing order of their corresponding values. For each element, calculate its contribution to the sum by determining the number of subarrays where it is the minimum.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through all possible subarrays, calculate their sum and range, then find the maximum difference.

steps.label: optimal
steps.desc: Use a monotonic stack to keep track of elements in increasing order. For each element, calculate its contribution to the sum and range by finding previous smaller and next greater elements.

steps.label: brute
steps.desc: Iterate through each digit and remove it, then find the minimum number from the remaining digits. Repeat k times.

steps.label: brute
